g.data-narration: Welcome to Concatenation of Transformations. This lecture covers combining multiple transformations efficiently using matrix multiplication.
g.text: Matrix Composition for Efficient Geometric Transformations

g.data-narration: Real graphics need multiple transformations: position, rotate, scale an object. Instead of applying each separately, we combine them into one matrix.
g.text: In real graphics, we need multiple transformations on objects:

g.data-narration: Concatenation combines multiple transformation matrices into one composite matrix using matrix multiplication, applied efficiently in one step.
g.text: Combining two or more transformation matrices into a SINGLE composite matrix
Composite M produces same result as applying each transformation sequentially

g.data-narration: Homogeneous coordinates represent 2D points as 3D vectors [x,y,1]. This enables all transformations to be expressed as matrix multiplications.
g.text: All transformations expressed as matrix multiplication using homogeneous coordinates
Enables ALL transforms as matrix mult

g.data-narration: Three fundamental 2D transformation matrices: Translation moves points, Rotation turns them around origin, Scaling changes their distance from origin.
Matrix multiplication is non-commutative: A × B ≠ B × A. In transformation M = A × B, the RIGHT matrix B applies FIRST, then A.
g.text: Matrix multiplication is NON-COMMUTATIVE: A × B ≠ B × A
In composite M = A × B: RIGHT matrix B is applied FIRST, then A

g.data-narration: Example: Translate by (2,0), then rotate 90°. M = T(2,0) × R(90°). The composite matrix transforms points correctly.
Reverse order R × T gives different result! Rotate first, then translate by (2,0) yields a completely different final position.
g.text: Now R(90°) × T(2,0): Rotate FIRST, then Translate

g.data-narration: To rotate about a fixed point P: translate P to origin, apply rotation, translate back. Three-step concatenation: T(P) × R × T(-P).
g.text: To rotate/scale about point P(px,py): use THREE-step concatenation

g.data-narration: 3D transformations use 4×4 matrices. The concepts are identical to 2D: concatenate by multiplication, last transformation applies first.
g.text: 3D uses 4×4 matrices in homogeneous coordinates. Same concatenation principles apply.

g.data-narration: Concatenation improves performance: apply one composite matrix instead of multiple matrices per vertex. Critical for real-time graphics.
g.text: Why concatenate? Three key performance advantages:
Apply 1 matrix vs N matrices per vertex
Matrix mult once during setup, not per vertex

g.data-narration: Example: Scale by (2,1), then rotate 90°. Using M = R × S composite, point (1,1) transforms to (-2,2).
g.text: Square (1,1), (3,1), (3,3), (1,3): Scale by 2 in x, then Rotate 90°

g.data-narration: Key formulas to remember: Translation, Rotation, Scaling matrices, Fixed Point formula M = T(P) × Transform × T(-P), and the Order Rule.
Exam tips: Remember the order rule, always include homogeneous coordinate w=1, use 4×4 for 3D, and practice solving numerical problems.
